summary: Jeff Atwood celebrates the announcement that Windows XP Media Center Edition 2005 will finally be available as a retail product, ending Microsoft's restriction of only selling it pre-installed on complete PCs. He argues this was a significant mistake that pushed enthusiasts toward alternatives like MythTV and Linux. Atwood shares his credentials as an HTPC enthusiast and highlights MCE's technical significance as one of Microsoft's first commercial products built entirely in .NET. He also discusses the unique challenges of designing '10 foot interfaces' for TV-based applications, noting that despite MCE's quality, TiVo still has superior UI design.
key_points: Microsoft's previous restriction of MCE to OEM-only distribution was a 'great wrong' that artificially limited an excellent product and drove users to competitors
MCE was one of Microsoft's first commercial products developed entirely in .NET, serving as an important proof of concept for the technology
Designing usable '10 foot interfaces' for home theater use is radically different from traditional GUI design and requires DirectX for acceptable performance
The HTPC enthusiast community developed strong alternatives (SageTV, MythTV, Freevo) specifically because Microsoft made MCE inaccessible
Despite being one of the best HTPC applications available, MCE's UI is still inferior to TiVo's
money_quotes: This rectifies a great wrong; the prior two editions of MCE were excellent mainstream products stymied into a tiny niche because nobody could get a copy!
Did you know that MCE was one of the first commercial products from MS developed entirely in .NET?
Putting together a usable '10 foot interface' is radically different from all my other GUI design experience.
You really have to bite the bullet and develop against DirectX if you want something that looks like MCE and performs at a decent speed.
In short, I'm a giant dork – but you knew that already.
And, really, what other logical use is there for 300gb+ hard drives, other than to fill them with video?
key_insight: Artificially restricting access to good software through distribution limitations only drives users to build or adopt alternatives, ultimately hurting the original product's market position.
